(2) the behavioral experiment is presented as 'direct evidence of faster convergence,' conflating optimization-theoretic rate with downstream dialogue quality, an invalid inferential step.","R1.3":"BehaviorCoherence@K is a human-rated Likert score for dialogue quality, not a measure of optimization convergence speed; the section's explicit claim that this constitutes 'direct evidence' for the O(1/√T) rate is a broken evidence-to-claim bridge and is load-bearing to the experimental narrative.","R1.4":"The entire theoretical contribution rests on the claim that O(1/√T) strictly improves on the prior state of the art; reviewing requires verifying whether Polyak (1967) is actually the tightest known bound for nonsmooth nonconvex stochastic optimization on a simplex, and whether the comparison regime is correctly matched.","R1.7":"The O(1/T^{1/4}) rate is attributed to Polyak (1967), but Polyak (1967) addresses convex stochastic approximation, not nonsmooth nonconvex problems; rationale.R1.1: The section's value rests on two explicit contribution claims—first sublinear-rate guarantee for memory-retrieval policy optimization, and an improvement over Polyak (1967)—so the thesis framing and gap statement require direct scrutiny.
rationale.R1.2: Two internal logical tensions are present: (1) the problem setup describes gradients as heavy-tailed, yet the convergence theorem invokes bounded-variance assumptions—these are in direct conflict; (2) the behavioral experiment is presented as 'direct evidence of faster convergence,' conflating optimization-theoretic rate with downstream dialogue quality, an invalid inferential step.
rationale.R1.3: BehaviorCoherence@K is a human-rated Likert score for dialogue quality, not a measure of optimization convergence speed; the section's explicit claim that this constitutes 'direct evidence' for the O(1/√T) rate is a broken evidence-to-claim bridge and is load-bearing to the experimental narrative.
rationale.R1.4: The entire theoretical contribution rests on the claim that O(1/√T) strictly improves on the prior state of the art; reviewing requires verifying whether Polyak (1967) is actually the tightest known bound for nonsmooth nonconvex stochastic optimization on a simplex, and whether the comparison regime is correctly matched.
rationale.R1.7: The O(1/T^{1/4}) rate is attributed to Polyak (1967), but Polyak (1967) addresses convex stochastic approximation, not nonsmooth nonconvex problems; the canonical nonsmooth nonconvex SGD rate appears in later literature (e.g., Ghadimi & Lan 2013), which is entirely absent, making this attribution likely inaccurate and the novelty claim unsupported.

parsed.clarification_question: Is this a substantive technical review (checking logical consistency, theoretical claims, and citation accuracy) or a writing/copyedit review (checking prose clarity and style)?
